parts.content: Discuss the benefits of mixed land use in urban planning.
parts.markscheme:
- Reduced Travel Needs: Residents can access work, shopping, and leisure facilities locally <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Community Cohesion: Mixed land use fosters vibrant, diverse communities <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Improved Quality of Life: Access to amenities increases residents' satisfaction <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Environmental Benefits: Reduces car dependency, lowering emissions <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Economic Opportunities: Increases business opportunities within neighborhoods <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Conclusion: Mixed land use contributes to sustainable urban development and improved livability <marks>1 mark</marks>

<marks>6 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Outline **four** ways that the conversion of primary forests to intensive agricultural land may influence processes in the carbon cycle.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of the following; <marks>4 marks max</marks>

- Removal of trees/biomass reduces the rate of photosynthesis $${\therefore}$$ reducing the amount of carbon dioxide ($$CO_2$$) sequestered from the atmosphere; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Burning of cleared vegetation (slash-and-burn) involves rapid combustion $${\therefore}$$ releasing stored organic carbon as $$CO_2$$ into the atmosphere; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Frequent tilling/ploughing of the soil increases aeration $${\therefore}$$ accelerating aerobic decomposition/microbial respiration of soil organic matter into $$CO_2$$; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Introduction of ruminant livestock (e.g., cattle) leads to enteric fermentation $${\therefore}$$ increasing the output of methane ($$CH_4$$) into the atmosphere; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Use of fossil-fuel-powered machinery for planting/harvesting involves combustion $${\therefore}$$ transferring long-term geological carbon stores to the atmospheric pool; <marks>1 mark</marks>


<Note>Ensure each markpoint includes a clear link between the agricultural activity and a specific process in the carbon cycle (e.g., photosynthesis, respiration, combustion). Award <marks>1 mark max</marks> for general statements about "pollution" without naming a specific carbon-based gas or process.</Note>

parts.content: Natural gas is a resource that can be exploited from geological reserves. Discuss how the value of this resource to a society may vary over time.
parts.markscheme:
_Positive changes promoting the value of natural gas_:  
- Natural gas may be seen as a "bridge fuel" by anthropocentric/technocentric thinkers because it produces lower $$CO_2$$ emissions than coal per unit of energy; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Technological advancements in hydraulic fracturing (fracking) have made previously inaccessible reserves economically viable, increasing its value as a source of energy security; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Economic crises or high inflation may increase the value of natural gas as a relatively cheap and abundant domestic energy source; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Shift in political priorities towards industrial growth may lead to deregulation, making natural gas extraction more profitable/attractive to investors; <marks>1 mark</marks>

_Negative changes reducing the value of natural gas_:  
- Increasing environmental awareness of "fugitive emissions" (methane leaks) during extraction may lead to an EVS shift that views natural gas as just as damaging as other fossil fuels; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Rapid cost reductions in renewable technologies (e.g., solar, wind) may render natural gas power plants less economically competitive; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Local social movements (e.g., anti-fracking campaigns) due to fears of groundwater contamination or seismic activity can decrease the societal acceptance/value of the resource; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Stricter international climate legislation (e.g., Net Zero targets) may mandate a complete transition away from all fossil fuels, including gas, reducing its long-term investment value; <marks>1 mark</marks>
- A shift toward deep ecocentrism may lead to the belief that fossil fuels should be "left in the ground" to respect the intrinsic value of the lithosphere/environment; <marks>1 mark</marks>

<Note> Award <marks>4 marks max</marks> if no balanced discussion present. For full marks, both positive and negative factors influencing the perceived value must be discussed, with clear links to EVS or societal context.</Note>

parts.content: To what extent are the impacts of global climate change better addressed through radical shifts in human lifestyle and consumption than through large-scale technocentric geoengineering?

labeledSubSubTopics.title: 7.1I Natural resource insecurity and security (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) focuses on how inequitable distribution of resources leads to conflict, which is a core theme in natural resource security and insecurity (Topic 7.1I).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 7: Natural resources > 7.1 Natural resources - uses and management > 7.1C Natural capital

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) directly asks for an outline of the reasons why natural capital is considered dynamic in nature, which is a specific syllabus requirement for Topic 7.1C.

parts.content: Outline the reasons why natural capital has a dynamic nature.
Explain how the inequitable distribution of natural resources can lead to conflict.
parts.markscheme:
- Natural resources like water/food/productive land/fossil fuel/ore deposits are distributed unequally around the globe, with some countries having an abundance while others face scarcity. This inequitable distribution can lead to conflict, as countries with limited resources may invade others to access their resources. For example, conflicts have arisen over ore deposits in Congo and Afghanistan. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Inequalities in resource distribution can also arise within societies due to factors such as overconsumption, population growth, lack of technology (agricultural/extraction/mining), or unsustainable development. Changes in surroundings, such as climatic change, international politics, economics, war, or embargoes, can further exacerbate these inequalities. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - For instance, the food price crisis in 2008 caused protests, riots, and political, economic, and social unrest in both less economically developed countries (LEDCs) and more economically developed countries (MEDCs). <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - Additionally, water scarcity due to climate change in Syria is argued to be a major cause of the civil war in 2011. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Conflicts often arise when a resource is shared across national borders, such as water storages or oil deposits. For example, the sharing of the Nile's water resources between Ethiopia, Sudan, and Egypt has been a source of tension. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Inequalities in energy and fuel reserves are particularly significant for a country's economics and national security, leading to and promulgating many conflicts in regions like the Middle East. <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Conflicts can also occur between constituencies within a country, such as social classes, ethnic groups, or resentments over government regulations, bans, taxation, or private ownership versus public ownership. For instance, conflicts may arise when the cost of clean water is prohibitive for lower social classes. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for any argument/valid example connecting unequal resource distribution to conflict. Allow a maximum of <marks>4 marks</marks> for outlining inequality in resource distribution without clear reference to how it leads to conflict.</note>

parts.content:
The management of a resource can impact the production of solid domestic waste.

To what extent have the three levels of the pollution management model been successfully applied to the management of solid domestic waste?
parts.markscheme:
_The following guide for using the markbands suggests certain features that may be offered in responses. The five headings coincide with the criteria given in each of the markbands (although “ESS terminology” has been conflated with“Understanding concepts”). This guide simply provides some possible inclusions and should not be seen as requisite or comprehensive. It outlines the kind of elements to look for when deciding on the appropriate markband and the specific mark within that band._

_Answers may include_:

*   **understanding concepts and terminology** of pollution management model and its “three levels” _ie_ A. altering human activity/reducing production, B. regulating/limiting release, C. clean-up/restoration, economic incentives/disincentives, legislation, emission standards, pollutant extraction, habitat restoration, solid domestic waste, e-waste, hazardous waste, reduce/reuse/recycle, landfills, incineration, composting, biodegradable/non-biodegradable, zero-waste, waste to energy, _etc_
*   **breadth in addressing and linking** different levels with each other and with relevant management strategies to each _ie_ A. educational campaigns/legislation for reduced packaging/non-biodegradable products, product longevity, _etc_ and B. promotion of reuse/recycling, composting, landfills, incineration, waste to energy schemes, _etc_ and C. landfill reclamation, litter collection, bioremediation, detoxification of hazardous waste, restocking,_etc_
*   **examples** of specific schemes _eg_ A. tax on plastic bags/waste collection (_eg_ Germany/Rwanda), San Francisco zero waste by 2020, rewards for low-waste manufacturing, B. government waste to energy schemes, sponsored recycling schemes, C. mining landfills to remove hazardous waste (_e.g._ Switzerland), clean-up schemes for Pacific Garbage Patch, _etc_
*   **balanced analysis** of the success or otherwise i.e. relative strengths & weaknesses of a range of strategies from all three levels of the pollution management model acknowledging relevant counterarguments/alternative viewpoints.
*   **a conclusion that is consistent with, and supported by, analysis and examples given** _eg_ “Logically, it must be most effective to manage waste at the first level which prevents problems arising but, due to the inevitable inertia in changing people’s perceptions, values and activities, more is currently being successfully achieved through the next two levels.” _NB This is only an example of a possible conclusion. Candidates’ conclusions do not have to agree._

_Please refer to paper 2 markbands, available under the 'your tests' tab > supplemental materials_

parts.content: Using named examples, distinguish between the concept of a “charismatic” (flagship) species and a keystone species.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for two valid examples (one of each kind):
- Charismatic/flagship species examples: Panda, Tiger
- Keystone species examples: Northern Spotted Owl, Sea Stars, Fig Trees, Sea Otters, Jaguar

Award <marks>3 max</marks> for valid points of distinction:

- Charismatic/flagship species are used to publicize/advertise conservation campaigns, stimulate public action, and raise economic support.
- They are selected because they appeal to humans or have ideological, cultural, or religious significance.
- Their value is primarily subjective/relative to a society rather than ecological.

- Keystone species have a disproportionately large effect on their environment, may determine the structure of an ecosystem, and have many other species dependent on them.
- Keystone species may be publicly unpopular, considered a threat to locals, or considered pests, and may be killed for fur or as trophies.
- They are identified through ecological, scientific, and objective study of their relationships with the entire ecosystem.

<note>While it is acceptable to award a mark for stating a discriminatory feature of just one kind of species (without referring to contrasting feature of the other), do not credit directly converse statements twice e.g. "flagship are X ...keystone are not X" would gain only 1 mark if X was valid.</note>

parts.content: Explain the role of **two** historical influences who shaped the development of the environmental movement.
parts.markscheme:
Rachel Carson – Author of Silent Spring (1962)
- Silent Spring documented/highlighted the problems caused by the widespread use of synthetic pesticides
- Focus was placed on the activities of chemical companies
- Explained impact of use of insecticides/pesticides on birds of prey
- Led to widespread awareness amongst (American) public of environmental issues/bioaccumulation/biomagnification
- Was a focal point for the social/environmental movements of the 1960s
- Inspired many other environmentalists
- Led to ban on DDT for agricultural uses
- Inspired the formation of the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency <marks>4 marks</marks>

Alternatively: 

- Fukushima Daiichi nuclear disaster of 2011
- A natural disaster/earthquake/tsunami led to the biggest nuclear disaster since Chernobyl at Fukushima Nuclear Power Plant
- Estimates vary about the number of people affected by the disaster/no direct deaths initially/over 600 deaths by workers/thousands with increased risk of cancers
- As the disaster was able to happen in a "developed" country like Japan, many societies came to the conclusion that nuclear power could not be "safe"
- This has led to increased public pressure to phase out nuclear power generation
- Germany sped up plans to close nuclear reactors/over 90 % of Italy voted against government plans to expand nuclear power/Switzerland also decided to phase out nuclear power <marks>3 marks</marks>

<note>Award [1 max] for correctly identifying/naming two historical influences.</note>
<note>Award credit for valid statements that describe the personality/event; explain how it has influenced the movement; and explain exactly what gave rise to the influence.</note>
<note>If more than two events/personalities are addressed credit only the highest-scoring two.</note>
<note>Award [4 max] for each explanation of how historical influences shaped the development of environmentalism up to max of 7 marks.</note>

parts.content: Discuss the role of environmental value systems in the protection of tropical biomes.
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include_:

*   **understanding concepts and terminology** of environmental value systems, tropical biomes, biodiversity, conservation, habitat v species approach, reserves, ecotourism, flagship species, sustainable development/exploitation, goods/services.
*   **breadth in addressing and linking** environmental value systems with each other and ecocentric values with biorights, wilderness reserves, restrained resource use, _etc_ and anthropocentric values with laws/regulations/quotas, public consultation/agreements, sustainable exploitation, ecotourism _etc_ and technocentric values with economic development, habitat restoration, economic value of natural goods and services, gene banks _etc_
*   **examples** of tropical biomes _e.g._ tropical forests, grasslands, savannas, lakes & rivers, coral reefs etc and environmental value systems _eg_ ecocentrism, anthropocentrism, technocentrism, _etc_ and habitat/species protection strategies _eg_ reserves, managed sustainable harvesting, ecotourism, fishing quotas, _etc_and value systems in specific tropical societies _eg_ rights of nature enshrined in constitutions of Ecuador/Bolivia, human rights to a healthy environment in laws of Costa Rica/Brazil, _etc_
*   **balanced analysis** of the ways in which different value systems are likely to impact/influence the protection of tropical biomes, acknowledging relevant counter-arguments/alternative viewpoints.
*   **a conclusion that is consistent with, and supported by, analysis and examples given*

| Marks | Level descriptor |
|-------|-----------------|
| 0 | The response does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below and is not relevant to the question. |
| 1–3 | The response contains:• minimal evidence of knowledge and understanding of ESS issues or concepts • fragmented knowledge statements poorly linked to the context of the question • some appropriate use of ESS terminology • no examples where required, or examples with insufficient explanation/relevance • superficial analysis that amounts to no more than a list of facts/ideas • judgments/conclusions that are vague or not supported by evidence/argument. |
| 4–6 | The response contains: • some evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • knowledge statements effectively linked to the context of the question • largely appropriate use of ESS terminology • some use of relevant examples where required, but with limited explanation • clear analysis that shows a degree of balance • some clear judgments/conclusions, supported by limited evidence/arguments. |
| 7–9 | The response contains: • substantial evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • a wide breadth of knowledge statements effectively linked with each other, and to the context of the question • consistently appropriate and precise use of ESS terminology • effective use of pertinent, well-explained examples, where required, showing some originality • thorough, well-balanced, insightful analysis • explicit judgments/conclusions that are well-supported by evidence/arguments and that include some critical reflection. |

parts.content: Explain whether lithium is renewable or non-renewable natural capital.
parts.markscheme:
Lithium is non-renewable natural capital because:

- <marks>1 mark</marks> Lithium does not regenerate, whereas renewable natural capital refers to natural resources that regenerate as fast as they are used.
- <marks>1 mark</marks> Lithium is an element and elements cannot be chemically changed into other elements. It was present when the planet was formed and the total amount of lithium present cannot change.

<note>The command term "explain" means students must give a detailed account, including reasons.</note>

<marks> 2  max</marks>

parts.content: Natural capital is valued for many different reasons. List two ways in which natural capital may be valued.
parts.markscheme:
### List two ways in which natural capital may be valued.

- <marks>1 mark</marks> Cultural
- <marks>1 mark</marks> Environmental
- <marks>1 mark</marks> Ethical
- <marks>1 mark</marks> Intrinsic
- <marks>1 mark</marks> Social
- <marks>1 mark</marks> Spiritual
- <marks>1 mark</marks> Technological
- <marks>1 mark</marks> Economic
- <marks>1 mark</marks> Aesthetic

<note>The command term "list" is just that - a list with no explanation needed.</note>

<note>Award <marks>1 mark</marks> each for any two of the following (<marks>2 marks max</marks>):</note>

parts.content: The status of natural capital can change over time. This means that something that may be of value now, may have not been in the past. Or something that may have been valuable in the past, may no longer be now. Using one example, explain how this is possible.
parts.markscheme:
<marks>1 mark</marks> Natural capital may stop being useful because of an alternative being available, for example, flint is no longer used to light fires or cork is no longer used as a bottle stopper.

<marks>1 mark</marks> Natural capital may become useful when we develop the technology to exploit it, for example, North sea oil could not be extracted until the 1960s or fracking now makes some oil reserves useful.

<marks>1 mark</marks> Natural capital may decrease in value due to the risks surrounding its use, for example, uranium which is highly energy efficient and environmentally friendly but also poses risks with its use and disposal.

<marks>2 marks max</marks>
<note>Award <marks>1 mark max</marks>  if no valid example is given</note>
<note>Accept other reasonable answers</note>

parts.content: Explain how the tragedy of the commons impacts non-renewable natural capital.
parts.markscheme:
- <marks>1 mark</marks> The tragedy of the commons leads to the overexploitation and depletion of non-renewable natural capital, as individuals act in their own self-interest to maximize their use of the resource without considering the long-term consequences.

- <marks>1 mark</marks> Since non-renewable natural capital is finite and cannot be replenished, the tragedy of the commons results in its rapid depletion, as individuals fail to consider the impact of their actions on the resource's sustainability.

<note>The command term "explain" means students must give a detailed account, including reasons.</note>

<marks>2 marks max</marks>

questions.specification:
_The resource booklet provides information on Algonquin Provincial Park in Canada. Use the resource booklet and your own studies to answer the following_.

**Figure 7(b): Fact file on beaver hunting**

*   By 1900 beavers had been hunted almost to extinction for their pelts (skin and fur).
*   The IUCN classify beavers as “least concern” on the Red List.
*   There are 19 registered trap lines in Algonquin Provincial Park where beavers may be trapped for their pelts by First Nation (indigenous) people.
*   All trappers must have a licence, and may only trap during the hunting season using ethical traps.
*   135 000 beavers are still trapped and killed in Canada each year.
*   The price for a beaver pelt was US\$19 in 2015, compared to US\$500 a pelt in 1940.
*   Many landowners trap beavers on their property because of the damage they cause. (Beavers cut timbers and their dams can create floods causing millions of dollars of economic damage.)

\[Source: adapted from www.couplesresort.ca, www.ontario.ca, www.sbaa.ca,  
www.ecology.info, www.tobyhemenway.com and http://acs7.cortland.edu\]

parts.content: Identify **four** distinct categories of ecosystem services, as defined by the Millennium Ecosystem Assessment, that provide 'natural income' for human societies.
parts.markscheme:
- Supporting services (e.g., primary production, soil formation, nutrient cycling) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Provisioning services (e.g., food, fresh water, timber, fiber, fuel) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Regulating services (e.g., climate regulation, flood regulation, water purification, pollination) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Cultural services (e.g., aesthetic, spiritual, educational, recreational benefits) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each correct category identified. Examples are not required but may be used to clarify the category.</note>

parts.content: Outline the sequence of procedures required to conduct a comprehensive Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) for a proposed urban development project.
parts.markscheme:
- **Screening**: Determine if the project requires an EIA based on local legislation/scale of impact. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Scoping**: Identify the key environmental issues and requirements that need to be addressed. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Baseline Study**: Conduct a detailed inventory of the existing environment (biotic and abiotic factors) before development starts. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Impact Prediction**: Use models or expert judgment to estimate the potential scale and duration of environmental changes. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Mitigation Planning**: Propose specific measures to avoid, reduce, or compensate for identified negative impacts. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Environmental Statement (Production)**: Compile all findings and mitigation strategies into a formal report for public and government review. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Monitoring and Auditing**: Establish a program to track actual impacts during and after construction to ensure compliance and effectiveness of mitigation. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each distinct procedural stage described, up to a maximum of <marks>7 marks</marks>.</note>

parts.content: To what extent can the **Ecological Footprint (EF)** be considered a more effective model for measuring the sustainability of a human population than a **Natural Capital** assessment?
parts.markscheme:
_Answers may include_:

*   **Understanding of concepts**: Ecological Footprint (the area of land and water required to sustainably provide all resources at the rate at which they are being consumed and to assimilate all waste); Natural Capital (the stock of natural resources that produces a sustainable yield of goods and services).
*   **Breadth of comparison**: EF as a single indicator of consumption vs. Natural Capital as a measurement of available stocks; national vs. global scales; use of biocapacity; ecological overshoot; non-renewable vs. renewable capital.
*   **Examples**: Comparison of EF between MEDCs and LEDCs; specific examples of natural capital (e.g., fossil fuels vs. timber); the role of technological advancement in changing EF; local depletion of natural capital despite a stable global EF.
*   **Balanced analysis**: 
    *   **Strengths of EF**: Simple, easy to communicate/compare between countries, highlights global inequality, clearly demonstrates overshoot (where consumption exceeds regeneration).
    *   **Weaknesses of EF**: Ignores many forms of pollution (e.g., toxics, plastics), assumes land is used for only one purpose, does not distinguish between sustainable and unsustainable land management within the footprint.
    *   **Strengths of Natural Capital**: More comprehensive for local resource management, accounts for both biotic and abiotic (non-renewable) stocks, useful for economic valuation/GDP greening.
    *   **Weaknesses of Natural Capital**: Harder to quantify in a single metric, difficult to account for the mobility of resources (trade).
*   **Conclusion**: A conclusion that is supported by the analysis provided. _e.g._, "While the Ecological Footprint is a superior tool for raising public awareness and showing global imbalances, Natural Capital assessments provide the detailed data necessary for local policy-making and managing specific resource stocks sustainably."

parts.content: Evaluate the role of a transition toward a circular economy in maintaining the integrity of natural capital while meeting the demands of a growing human population.
parts.markscheme:
- <marks>1 mark</marks> for clear definitions and use of terminology: natural capital (the stock of resources), natural income (the yield/flow from that stock), linear economy (take-make-dispose), and circular economy (restorative and regenerative by design).

- <marks>1-2 marks</marks> for breadth in addressing the mechanisms of a circular economy:
  - Decoupling economic growth from resource consumption.
  - Designing out waste and pollution (e.g., closed-loop systems).
  - Keeping products and materials in use (reuse, remanufacture, recycling).
  - Regenerating natural systems instead of just extracting from them.

- <marks>1-2 marks</marks> for addressing the preservation of natural capital:
  - Reducing the rate of depletion of non-renewable stocks.
  - Ensuring harvesting rates of renewable resources do not exceed natural income/sustainable yield.
  - Reducing the 'waste' component of the ecological footprint, preventing ecosystem degradation.

- <marks>1 mark</marks> for specific examples/strategies:
  - Product-as-a-service models (e.g., leasing rather than owning appliances).
  - Biological cycles (composting/anaerobic digestion returning nutrients to soil).
  - Technical cycles (upcycling rare earth metals from electronics).

- <marks>1-2 marks</marks> for balanced analysis of challenges and limitations:
  - The Second Law of Thermodynamics (energy is always lost as heat, and materials degrade over time/recycling cycles).
  - High initial costs and the need for global systemic change in infrastructure.
  - Jevons Paradox (increased efficiency may lead to increased total consumption).
  - Difficulty in tracking international supply chains.

- <marks>1 mark</marks> for a conclusion supported by the analysis, e.g., "While a circular economy offers a significant pathway to sustainability by mimicking natural nutrient cycles, its success depends heavily on global cooperation and the ability to reduce absolute consumption levels, rather than just increasing efficiency."

parts.content: Outline **four** distinct categories of ecosystem services identified by the Millennium Ecosystem Assessment (MA) that provide natural income to humanity.
parts.markscheme:
*   **Provisioning services:** The products obtained from ecosystems, such as food, fresh water, timber, fiber, and genetic resources.
*   **Regulating services:** The benefits obtained from the regulation of ecosystem processes, such as climate regulation, flood control, water purification, and pollination.
*   **Cultural services:** Non-material benefits people obtain through spiritual enrichment, cognitive development, recreation, and aesthetic experiences.
*   **Supporting services:** Services necessary for the production of all other ecosystem services, such as soil formation, nutrient cycling, and primary production.

<Note> Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each of the following categories and a brief description; <marks>4 marks max</marks>

parts.content: Explain the process and limitations of using Environmental Impact Assessments (EIAs) as a tool for ensuring the sustainable management of a proposed large-scale development project.
parts.markscheme:
- <marks>1 mark</marks> for the initial stage: Establishing a baseline study to assess the existing environmental conditions (abiotic and biotic) before development begins.
- <marks>1 mark</marks> for predicting impacts: Identifying potential positive and negative effects on the environment, society, and economy.
- <marks>1 mark</marks> for mitigation: Proposing measures to avoid, reduce, or compensate for negative impacts (e.g., creating habitat corridors or using water filtration).
- <marks>1 mark</marks> for decision-making: Providing a formal document that allows authorities to decide if the project should proceed based on sustainability criteria.
- <marks>1 mark</marks> for a limitation regarding data/prediction: Complexities in ecosystems make it difficult to predict long-term or indirect effects (e.g., bioaccumulation or trophic cascades).
- <marks>1 mark</marks> for a limitation regarding implementation: Lack of standardization across borders or inadequate post-development monitoring to ensure mitigation steps are followed.
- <marks>1 mark</marks> for a limitation regarding bias/influence: Developers may exert political or economic pressure on the assessment process, or the public's voice may be ignored in favor of economic gain.

<note>Award [4 max] if only the process is described or only limitations are described. A full 7 marks requires a balance of how the tool works and why it might fail to ensure sustainability.</note>

parts.content:
To what extent does the management of natural capital, by limiting use to the natural income generated, ensure the long-term sustainability of a society?

In your response, include references to specific examples of natural capital.
parts.markscheme:
### Understanding Concepts and Terminology
- **Natural capital**: Natural resources that can produce a sustainable natural income of goods or services (e.g., a forest, a stock of fish). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Natural income**: The yield, harvest, or services obtained from natural capital (e.g., timber, annual catch). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Sustainability**: The use and management of resources that allows full replenishment of the resources exploited and full recovery of the ecosystems affected by their extraction and use. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Sustainable development**: Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Breadth in Addressing and Linking
- Links the exploitation of natural capital to the depletion of the 'standing stock' if income is exceeded. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links sustainable management to the maintenance of ecosystem services (e.g., carbon sequestration, water purification). <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Examples
- **Example 1**: Overfishing (e.g., Bluefin tuna or North Sea Cod) where harvesting exceeds natural income, leading to stock collapse. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Example 2**: Sustainable forestry (e.g., FSC certified logging) where harvest rates match regrowth rates. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Example 3**: Groundwater extraction (e.g., Ogallala Aquifer) where extraction exceeds recharge. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Balanced Analysis
- **Supporting argument**: Limiting use to income prevents the 'Tragedy of the Commons' and ensures resources are available for the future. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Counter-argument/Complexity**: It is difficult to quantify 'natural income' accurately due to dynamic environmental variables; some natural capital is non-renewable (e.g., minerals/fossil fuels) and has no 'natural income' in human timescales, meaning any use is inherently unsustainable. <marks>2 marks</marks>

### Conclusion
- Conclusion consistent with the analysis. <marks>1 mark</marks>
  - *Example*: While limiting use to natural income is the theoretical basis for sustainability, it is practically difficult to measure, and modern societies still rely heavily on non-renewable capital which cannot be managed as 'income'.

<note>Total marks: 9 marks (Max 9 from points above).</note>

parts.content: Outline **four** specific factors that would cause a country’s ecological footprint to be significantly higher than the global average.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each distinct factor identified and outlined; <marks>4 marks max</marks>.

- **High meat consumption**: Diets rich in meat require significantly more land area for grain production and grazing compared to plant-based diets. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Reliance on fossil fuels**: High carbon dioxide emissions require large areas of vegetation (forests) to sequester the carbon, increasing the 'carbon' component of the footprint. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **High levels of industrialization/consumption**: Results in high energy demands and high waste production, which increases the land required for resource extraction and waste assimilation. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Low population density/High affluence**: Leads to greater per capita use of private transport and larger housing, which consumes more energy and land resources. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Low efficiency of resource use**: Use of outdated technology or lack of recycling infrastructure leads to higher waste and higher demand for raw materials per unit of output. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Import-intensive economy**: Relying on goods transported from far away increases the carbon footprint due to transport emissions. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
Environmental Impact Assessments (EIAs) are intended to support sustainable development.

Evaluate the effectiveness of an EIA in ensuring the sustainability of a named development project.
parts.markscheme:
### Named Project
- Identification of a specific project (e.g., Three Gorges Dam, HS2 Rail, local housing development). <note>If no project is named, or project is generic, max 4 marks.</note>

### Analysis of Effectiveness (Points for/against)
- **Baseline studies**: Provides a starting point to measure future environmental change; allows for the identification of sensitive species or habitats before damage occurs. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Mitigation strategies**: Suggests ways to reduce impact (e.g., building 'wildlife bridges' or replanting forest elsewhere), which can make a project more sustainable. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Decision-making tool**: Informs local authorities/governments, potentially leading to the rejection of the most damaging proposals. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Public participation**: Can provide a platform for local stakeholders to voice concerns, though this varies by country. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Limitations
- **Lack of standardisation**: Guidelines vary globally; some countries may have very weak requirements for what constitutes a 'significant' impact. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Economic/Political pressure**: Governments may ignore EIA findings if the economic benefits of a project are high (e.g., prioritising short-term jobs over long-term ecology). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Difficulty in prediction**: Ecological systems are complex; EIAs may fail to predict indirect or cumulative effects (e.g., downstream impacts of a dam). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Bias**: The scientists/consultants conducting the EIA are often paid by the developer, which can lead to a conflict of interest/underestimation of impacts. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Lack of post-project monitoring**: Many EIAs are only done *before* construction; there is often no legal requirement to check if predictions were accurate or if mitigation worked. <marks>1 mark</marks>

### Conclusion
- A summary statement reflecting on whether the EIA actually made the named project 'sustainable' or merely 'less damaging'. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award 1 mark for each valid evaluative point, up to 7 marks max.</note>

parts.content: Outline how nuclear energy is an example of the dynamic nature of a resource.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> each for any four of the following (maximum <marks>4 marks</marks>):

- Before the 1940s/1950s, nuclear energy/uranium was not considered an energy resource.
- In the 1950s, the first nuclear power plants were built/nuclear energy was developed.
- Nuclear energy started becoming increasingly popular because of its efficiency/because it is clean/does not contribute to global warming.
- However, after Chernobyl (1986)/Fukushima (2011), more people started questioning the safety of nuclear energy in general/in earthquake areas.
- Nowadays, many believe it is a solution to help mitigate climate change.
- Others consider it dangerous/not an option because of earthquakes/wars (Ukraine - risk of bombardment of nuclear power plant)/nuclear waste/it is non-renewable/mining for uranium degrades the environment.

parts.content: Explain how an enhanced greenhouse effect can lead to climate change.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> each for any of the following (maximum <marks>7 marks</marks>):

- GHE keeps the Earth warm/makes life on Earth possible.
- The temperature of the Earth depends on the concentration of GHGs in the atmosphere.
- GHGs, such as $\text{CO}_2$, methane, $\text{NO}_x$, and CFCs, prevent heat/infrared/longer wavelength radiation from leaving the atmosphere.
- Human activities, such as burning fossil fuels/deforestation/industrialisation/transportation, increase the concentration of GHGs.
- Increased concentration of GHGs means that more heat is trapped/prevented from leaving the Earth/from going out into space/leaving the atmosphere.
- This leads to global warming/increase in temperatures especially at the poles.
- This causes changes in the world's weather patterns/ocean circulation/wind patterns.
- This leads to an increase in the frequency and severity of climatic events/climate-related events such as heat waves, droughts, rains winds/hurricanes.

parts.content: To what extent are the individual actions of each country more important than global actions when dealing with climate change?
parts.markscheme:
### Markband Descriptors

<marks>0 marks</marks>: The response does not reach a standard described by the descriptors below and is not relevant to the question.

<marks>1–3 marks</marks>: The response contains:
- Minimal evidence of knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts
- Fragmented knowledge statements that are poorly linked to the context of the question
- Some appropriate use of ESS terminology
- No examples, where required, or examples with insufficient explanation/relevance
- Superficial analysis that amounts to no more than a list of facts/ideas
- Judgements/conclusions that are vague or not supported by evidence/argument

<marks>4–6 marks</marks>: The response contains:
- Some evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts
- Knowledge statements that are effectively linked to the context of the question
- Largely appropriate use of ESS terminology
- Some use of relevant examples, where required, but with limited explanation
- Clear analysis that shows a degree of balance
- Some clear judgements/conclusions that are supported by limited evidence/arguments

<marks>7–9 marks</marks>: The response contains:
- Substantial evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts
- A wide breadth of knowledge statements that are effectively linked to each other and to the context of the question
- Consistent, appropriate and precise use of ESS terminology
- Effective use of pertinent, well-explained examples, where required, showing some originality
- Thorough, well-balanced, insightful analysis
- Explicit judgements/conclusions that are well supported by evidence/arguments and that include some critical reflection

<note>In order to do well in this question, students must assess the extent to which individual actions are the best option to address climate change or NOT. Notice that the question says "are more important". Students may agree with the statement. However, they should ask themselves whether individual actions or global actions are more important, or whether BOTH are equally important.</note>

parts.content: Outline **two** ecosystem services in a named biome.
parts.markscheme:
**Tundra**

- Permafrost/glaciers in tundra provide important storage in hydrological cycle <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Ice in tundra provides reflective surface/increases planetary albedo, thus moderating global temperatures <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Wetlands**

- Decomposers/high productivity in swamps/wetlands provides filtration of inorganic nutrients / water purification <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Storage of water in wetlands prevents flooding / provides ideal resting grounds for migratory birds <marks>1 mark</marks>

#### Tropical rainforests

- High biodiversity in tropical rainforests promotes ecotourism/recreation <marks>1 mark</marks>  
- High rate of photosynthesis in tropical rainforests maintains balance of O2/CO2 in atmosphere <marks>1 mark</marks>

#### Boreal/temperate forests

- Tree populations in boreal/temperate forests prevent soil erosion on mountainsides <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Forest canopies in forests provide shade/shelter/habitat for diversity of species <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Be careful only to credit "services" (maintenance/establishment of favourable conditions) and not "goods" (consumable/harvestable/physical products).</note>  

<note>Award **[2 max]** if no biome is identified. If more than one biome is given, credit only highest scoring biome addressed.</note>

parts.content: Explain the causes, and the possible consequences, of the loss of a named critically endangered species.
parts.markscheme:
### Causes <marks>3 max</marks>

- Habitat loss / deforestation
- Habitat degradation / pollution
- Narrowly distributed / endemic
- Poaching / overhunting
- Illegal trafficking of species
- Disease
- Small population size/gene pool / inbreeding
- Specialised niche
- Slow reproduction rate / specialised reproductive behaviour
- High trophic level/top predator
- Low/negative cultural value
- Influence/competition/predation from invasive species

### Consequences <marks>3 max</marks>

- Loss of an aesthetically attractive organism
- Loss of ethically significant life / breach of biorights
- Loss of biodiversity
- Increase in organisms upon which the species fed or competed with
- Decline in other organisms due to loss of food source
- If keystone species, widespread impacts/cascade effects on food chains/ecosystem
- Economic costs from loss of ecosystem services provided by the species
- Economic costs from loss of tourism opportunities
- Social impacts on local culture as important/significant cultural loss

<note>Max. 4 marks without explaination </note>

parts.content: Using examples, discuss whether habitat conservation is more successful than a species-based approach to protecting threatened species.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include: 
- Habitat conservation aims to protect entire ecosystems and the various species within them, while a species-based approach focuses on specific threatened species.
- Protects multiple species and their interactions within an ecosystem 
- Addresses root causes of biodiversity loss like habitat destruction and fragmentation 
- Maintains ecosystem services like water purification, nutrient cycling, and climate regulation 
- Examples: Establishing protected areas, restoring degraded habitats, sustainable forestry practices 
- Targets critically endangered species at high risk of extinction 
- Allows for focused efforts on specific species' needs and threats 
- Examples: Captive breeding programs, reintroduction efforts, anti-poaching measures

| 4–6 | The response contains: • some evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • knowledge statements effectively linked to the context of the question • largely appropriate use of ESS terminology • some use of relevant examples where required, but with limited explanation • clear analysis that shows a degree of balance • some clear judgments/conclusions, supported by limited evidence/arguments. |
| 7–9 | The response contains: • substantial evidence of sound knowledge and understanding of ESS issues and concepts • a wide breadth of knowledge statements effectively linked with each other, and to the context of the question • consistently appropriate and precise use of ESS terminology • effective use of pertinent, well-explained examples, where required, showing some originality • thorough, well-balanced, insightful analysis • explicit judgments/conclusions that are well-supported by evidence/arguments and that include some critical reflection. |

questions.specification:
**Figure 1: Demographic indicators for a developing nation (1995 and 2025 predicted)**

| Indicator | 1995 | 2025 (Projected) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Crude birth rate (CBR) | 42.0 per 1000 | 21.0 per 1000 |
| Crude death rate (CDR) | 14.0 per 1000 | 7.0 per 1000 |
| Total fertility rate (TFR) | 5.8 | 2.4 |
| Female secondary education enrollment | 18% | 65% |

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 8: Human populations and urban systems > 8.1 Human populations  > 8.1A Birth and Immigration as Inputs to Population Growth
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 8.1A Birth and Immigration as Inputs to Population Growth
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) requires calculating the natural increase rate (NIR) and doubling time using crude birth rate (CBR) and crude death rate (CDR), which are primary inputs to population growth.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 8: Human populations and urban systems > 8.1 Human populations  > 8.1B Human population management
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 8.1B Human population management
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) focuses on how female education enrollment impacts total fertility rates, which is a key social factor used in human population management and understanding population dynamics.

parts.content: Using the data in Figure 1, calculate the natural increase rate (NIR) for the year 1995 and determine the doubling time for the population at that rate.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for the correct NIR calculation and <marks>1 mark</marks> for the correct doubling time calculation.

- Natural Increase Rate (NIR) for 1995: $$\frac{\text{42.0 - 14.0} }{10}= 2.8\%$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Doubling Time for 1995: $$\frac{70}{2.8} = 25 \text{ years}$$ <marks>1 mark</marks>


<note>Accept calculations using the formula $$\frac{\text{CBR} - \text{CDR}}{10}$$ for NIR and $$\frac{70}{\text{NIR}}$$ for doubling time. Units must be correct (percentage for NIR, years for doubling time) for full marks.

parts.content: With reference to the data in Figure 1, explain how the change in female education enrollment may have contributed to the projected shift in the total fertility rate (TFR) by 2025.

parts.content:
The following table shows the energy consumption profiles of two anonymous countries, Country X and Country Y.

| Energy Source | Country X (% of total) | Country Y (% of total) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Coal | 62 | 5 |
| Natural Gas | 18 | 25 |
| Nuclear | 0 | 42 |
| Renewables (Hydro, Wind, Solar) | 20 | 28 |

Explain **four** factors (physical, economic, or political) that may account for the differences in the energy choices made by these two countries. <marks>4 marks</marks>
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each distinct factor explained with reference to the table:

- **Physical/Geographical availability:** Country X may have large domestic reserves of coal, whereas Country Y may lack fossil fuel deposits but have the geography suitable for high nuclear or gas imports. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Economic factors/Level of development:** Country Y may be a wealthier nation able to afford the high capital costs of nuclear infrastructure, while Country X relies on coal which is often cheaper and utilizes older, established infrastructure. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Political/Energy Security goals:** Country Y may have a policy to reduce greenhouse gas emissions or dependency on coal, leading to a higher nuclear/gas share, whereas Country X may prioritize low-cost energy for industrial growth. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Technological expertise:** Country Y may possess the advanced scientific and technical workforce required to safely manage a large nuclear sector, while Country X may focus on simpler extraction technologies. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Environmental legislation:** Country Y may have implemented strict carbon taxes or signed international agreements that make coal use prohibitively expensive, unlike Country X. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Do not award marks for simply stating the data from the table; the response must explain the *reason* for the difference.</note>
<note>Accept any other valid explanations of factors influencing energy mix.</note>

parts.content: The exploitation of unconventional energy sources, such as shale gas (via hydraulic fracturing) or tar sands, is often promoted as a solution to energy insecurity. Discuss the environmental and economic trade-offs associated with this strategy. <marks>7 marks</marks>
parts.markscheme:
Award marks for points discussing both positive (security/economic) and negative (environmental) aspects. 

**Economic/Security Benefits (Max 4 marks):**
- **Domestic Supply:** Reduces dependence on energy imports from politically unstable regions, increasing national sovereignty. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Price Stability:** Increased supply can lead to lower and more stable domestic energy prices for consumers and industry. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Job Creation:** Exploration and extraction of unconventional sources create significant local employment and stimulate the economy. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Bridge Fuel:** Natural gas from fracking produces less $$CO_2$$ per unit of energy than coal, potentially acting as a transition fuel. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Environmental Trade-offs (Max 4 marks):**
- **Water Contamination:** The chemicals used in fracking or the tailings ponds in tar sands can leak into local groundwater/aquifers. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **High Water Consumption:** These processes require vast quantities of freshwater, which may compete with agricultural or domestic needs. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Methane Leakage:** Extraction and transport of shale gas often involve significant fugitive methane emissions (a potent greenhouse gas). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Habitat Destruction:** Tar sands extraction involves large-scale surface mining, leading to the total removal of overlying ecosystems/boreal forests. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Induced Seismicity:** The injection of wastewater from fracking has been linked to increased frequency of minor earthquakes. <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>A maximum of <marks>7 marks</marks> total can be awarded. Ensure the response addresses both economic/security benefits and environmental costs for full marks.</note>

parts.content: Evaluate the role of national energy policies in balancing the conflict between economic development and environmental sustainability, with reference to two named countries. <marks>9 marks</marks>
parts.markscheme:
Evaluation should consider how the level of development and domestic priorities shape energy choices and the resulting impact on sustainability.

**Example 1: China (Rapidly Industrializing/Emerging Economy)**
- **Policy:** China has historically prioritized coal to fuel rapid economic growth and urbanization (energy equity/growth). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Conflict:** This led to severe air pollution and high global $$CO_2$$ emissions, compromising environmental sustainability. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Shift:** Recent policies have made China the world leader in wind and solar investment to mitigate health costs and meet international climate commitments (balancing act). <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Example 2: Germany (Developed Economy - Energiewende)**
- **Policy:** The *Energiewende* aimed for a massive shift to renewables and a phase-out of nuclear power for environmental safety. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Conflict:** This has led to high electricity prices for citizens (impacting energy equity) and a temporary increase in coal use to maintain grid stability after nuclear shutdowns. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Outcome:** While sustainability has improved through carbon reduction, the policy faces challenges in maintaining industrial competitiveness due to energy costs. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Example 3: Iceland (Geographically Favored)**
- **Policy:** Utilizing 100% renewable domestic heat and power (hydro/geothermal). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Outcome:** High energy security and sustainability achieved simultaneously, though this is heavily dependent on unique physical geography rather than policy alone. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Evaluation/Conclusion:**
- National policies are often dictated by the "Energy Trilemma" (security, equity, sustainability). <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Lower-income nations often prioritize development and equity (cheap fossil fuels), while higher-income nations have the capital to prioritize sustainability. <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Transitioning energy systems is slow due to the "infrastructure lock-in" of existing fossil fuel plants. <marks>1 mark</marks>

**Award marks according to the following levels:**
- **[7–9 marks]** for a well-balanced evaluation referencing two named countries, clearly identifying the trade-offs between development and sustainability, and providing a reasoned conclusion.
- **[4–6 marks]** for a discussion that identifies conflicts in one or two countries but lacks depth in evaluation or has a less balanced approach.
- **[1–3 marks]** for a descriptive response that lacks specific country examples or only addresses one side of the conflict.

questions.specification:
**Figure 1: Taiga**

*   Taiga is the world’s largest terrestrial biome.
*   The Siberian taiga contains about 20 % of the world’s forested areas.
*   The taiga includes evergreen trees such as pine, spruce, fir, and some deciduous trees such as birch and poplar.
*   Nutrient-poor soil and the climatic conditions contribute to slow rate of tree growth.
*   Threats to taiga include forest fires, clearance for agriculture land, logging for timber, mining, road building and climate change.
*   The region suffers deforestation rates of 20000km2 annually.
*   Conservation measures include restoration of forests through replanting and designating protected areas such as National Parks and selective logging policies.
*   There is high global demand for timber with markets throughout Asia, Europe and the USA. Economic incentives to increase exports have increased timber production, destroying more of the forest ecosystems.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 7: Natural resources > 7.1 Natural resources - uses and management > 7.1B Ecosystem services
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 7.1B Ecosystem services
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) asks for an ecological service provided by the taiga, which directly targets ecosystem services.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.3 Biogeochemical cycles > 2.3B Carbon cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.3B Carbon cycle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Deforestation affecting the world’s oceans is most plausibly via increased atmospheric CO2/reduced carbon sequestration, linking terrestrial forests to the carbon cycle and ocean uptake.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 4: Water > 4.1 Water systems  > 4.1C Oceans as a carbon sink (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 4.1C Oceans as a carbon sink (HL)
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (b) explicitly connects land deforestation to impacts on the oceans, commonly explained through altered CO2 levels and oceans’ role as a carbon sink (ocean acidification).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 3: Conservation and biodiversity > 3.3 Conservation and regeneration > 3.3B Regeneration and rewilding
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 3.3B Regeneration and rewilding
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) concerns restoration through replanting and what limits regeneration, fitting regeneration/restoration concepts.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Topic 2: Ecology > 2.4 Climate and biomes > 2.4B Biomes
labeledSubSubTopics.title: 2.4B Biomes
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The stimulus and questions are set in the taiga biome context (climate/soil constraints), so understanding biomes underpins limiting factors and services.

parts.content: Outline **one** ecological service provided by the Siberian taiga.
Explain how deforestation in the taiga may impact the world’s oceans.
State **one** natural limiting factor to the successful restoration of taiga through replanting.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for any of the following (<marks>1 max</marks>):

nutrient poor soils / low temperature / low rainfall / limited growing season / limited amount of sunlight / slow growth rates / permafrost inhibits plant growth;

_Do not accept ‘wildfires / grazers/animals eat the plants/seedlings’._  
_Do not accept just ‘temperature/rainfall/soil fertility/time it takes for trees to grow/long mature time for trees’._
